_cite.reason: URL-normalization 1c: strip trailing markdown-emphasis `*`/backtick glued to citation URLs in intelligence_item_sections.content_md (customer-checkable link + validate_item_provenance criterion-2 exact-compare). Operator ruling 2026-07-04. Same transform as the write-site fix.

prior.content_md:
The EPA Endangerment Finding repeal and the parallel federal-California legal conflict create a layered compliance chain in which the workspace, as a non-asset freight forwarder, sits as a 'contracting principal' between beneficial cargo owners (BCOs) and asset-owning surface and air carriers. The workspace does not itself register vehicles or hold EPA certification obligations; however, it bears contractual and reputational exposure through its selection, vetting, and instruction of subcontracted road carriers whose vehicles must be certified under whichever vehicle emissions standard is operative in the jurisdiction of haul.

Under the repealed federal GHG framework, EPA's MY2012–MY2027+ vehicle GHG standards provided a single national compliance floor. That floor has now been eliminated at the federal level, with EPA asserting it lacked statutory authority under Clean Air Act §202(a) to issue such standards. In parallel, CARB has permanently re-anchored California compliance to earlier (pre-ACC II) CARB standards via its Emergency Vehicle Emissions Regulation. The workspace's ground-transport subcontractors operating in California — critical for cargo movement into the Los Angeles and San Francisco hub markets that dominate live events, fine art, and luxury goods verticals — must now demonstrate certification to these CARB legacy standards rather than any federal GHG standard.

For air cargo operations, the repeal does not directly affect aircraft emissions, which are regulated under separate FAA and ICAO frameworks. However, ground-side emissions requirements at airports in California and Section 177 states remain subject to CARB authority where waivers are not preempted. For ocean freight, port-area truck access in California (e.g., Port of Los Angeles drayage) is governed by CARB's existing port drayage rules, whose waiver status under the Congressional Review Act resolutions signed June 2025 is under active litigation.

The workspace's role-placement requiring legal confirmation: whether the workspace, as the contracting party issuing transport orders and specifying carrier qualifications, could bear secondary liability in California for instructing or procuring services from carriers not certified to operative CARB standards. Legal review of subcontractor qualification clauses is advised before the D.C. Circuit issues any preliminary ruling.

*Source: EPA Final Rule rescinding 2009 Endangerment Finding, U.S. EPA, February 12–18, 2026. https://www.epa.gov/newsreleases/president-trump-and-administrator-zeldin-deliver-single-largest-deregulatory-action-us; California Governor's Office Lawsuit Announcement, March 19, 2026. https://www.gov.ca.gov/2026/03/19/california-is-taking-donald-trump-to-court-for-breaking-the-law-to-put-polluter-profits-before-american-lives/; CARB Emergency Vehicle Emissions Regulation, CARB, March 26, 2026. https://ww2.arb.ca.gov/news/carb-approves-permanent-adoption-emergency-vehicle-emissions-regulation*
